title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
author: Holden Diffner
orientation: Holden Diffner unpacks Pastor Blake Gideon's claim that the debate between immediatism and incrementalism is fundamentally about whether the church bows before the holiness of God. He argues that a renewed vision of God's holy commands consumes pragmatism and forces Christians to demand the immediate establishment of justice for the preborn rather than incremental pro-life compromises.
audiences: Pro-life Christian who defends incremental legislation
Pastor or church leader weighing the abolition vs. pro-life divide
Abolitionist sharpening the theological case against incrementalism
Skeptic suspicious of appeals to divine authority in policy debate
New Christian wrestling with how God's holiness shapes ethics

questions.question: Why does Diffner say the immediatism vs. incrementalism debate is not really about clarity of argument?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian who defends incremental legislation
questions.answer: Because, in Diffner's framing of Blake Gideon's point, God has already spoken on the matter. The dispute therefore is not whether the Bible is unclear but whether the disputant is willing to submit to God's revealed holiness rather than negotiate around it.
questions.quote: There's no issue for debate between immediatism and incrementalism when God has already spoken. The issue is not clarity on the topic–the issue is the holiness of God, and whether or not you bow before the holiness of God.

questions.question: How does the article define holiness, and why does that definition matter for the abolition argument?
questions.audience: New Christian wrestling with how God's holiness shapes ethics
questions.answer: Holiness means 'set apart'—utterly other, separate, and beyond human grasp. Diffner uses this otherness to insist that God's commands cannot be domesticated to fit human pragmatism; they stand on their own weight and demand conformity rather than negotiation.
questions.quote: This is not surprising, given that holiness is literally the most other-worldly word that can be uttered. It does, after all, mean “set apart”–separate, completely different, other than.

questions.question: What does Diffner say God's holiness actually does to creation?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader weighing the abolition vs. pro-life divide
questions.answer: He argues God's holiness is not Deistic distance but active, exclusive presence. It either annihilates what is unholy or, through Christ, produces complete conformity in the redeemed.
questions.quote: God's separate otherness is exclusive, and *actively so*. God is separate, and He is transcendent, but He is not distant–He is present. His holiness proceeds forth from Him and affects everything in existence.

questions.question: How does Isaiah's response in Isaiah 6 model the obedience Diffner is calling for?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader weighing the abolition vs. pro-life divide
questions.answer: Once cleansed, Isaiah responds without question or qualification, ready to obey before he even hears the assignment. Diffner uses this as the template for how Christians should respond to God's command to establish justice—immediately, not incrementally.
questions.quote: He didn't ask any questions. In fact, God hadn't even told him what his mission was yet. Isaiah was immediately ready to do whatever God was going to tell him.

questions.question: What role does pragmatism play in incrementalism, according to the article?
questions.audience: Abolitionist sharpening the theological case against incrementalism
questions.answer: Diffner says pragmatism is the engine of incrementalism—a 'cunning arithmetic' that calculates apparent good while ignoring God's clear command. Under the weight of God's holiness, that arithmetic is incinerated as chaff.
questions.quote: Pragmatism is now ruled out. Pragmatism is blown apart like straw before the incomprehensible weightiness of God's glory. It is incinerated like chaff before the consuming fire of God's holiness–namely, by His holy command to establish justice.

questions.question: Which specific pro-life measures does the article classify as INjustice?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian who defends incremental legislation
questions.answer: Diffner names parental consent initiatives, pain-capable unborn child protection acts, and heartbeat bills as examples of incremental laws that fall short of justice and therefore, in his view, establish injustice rather than abolish it.
questions.quote: Of course, the pragmatism we speak of here is the one that very many use to argue for and justify the establishment of INjustice (which is anything short of justice, including parental consent initiatives, pain-capable unborn child protection acts, heartbeat bills, and the like), contrary to God's law, on the altar of “success” as this incrementalist sees it.

questions.question: Why does Diffner think incrementalists rarely cite Scripture to defend their position?
questions.audience: Skeptic suspicious of appeals to divine authority in policy debate
questions.answer: Because, he argues, love of God's holy commands and worldly pragmatism are mutually exclusive. If incrementalism rests on pragmatic calculation, it cannot legitimately appeal to Scripture as its foundation.
questions.quote: It is interesting to note that one is hard pressed to find the incrementalist appealing to Scripture as the basis for his incrementalism. This, however, is not surprising in light of our discussion showing that love of God's holy commands and worldly pragmatism do not mix.

questions.question: What is the practical prescription the article ends with?
questions.audience: Abolitionist sharpening the theological case against incrementalism
questions.answer: A renewed vision of God's holiness producing fresh love for His commands, which then yields a fixed, unwavering pursuit of justice that no circumstance or political expediency can warp.
questions.quote: What we need here is a renewed vision of the holiness of God, flowing from it a new love for the commands of God, and therefore a fixation on establishing justice that won't be warped by any winds of circumstance or expediency.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Companion piece from the same site giving the concise framing of the very debate Diffner says God has already settled.

related.title: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
related.reason: Silberman's parallel theological argument that incremental laws blunt the call to repentance the holiness of God demands.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Provides the concrete policy-level objections that complement Diffner's purely theological critique.

related.title: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
related.reason: Documents the Oklahoma Baptist context (and Blake Gideon's involvement) that frames the Rescue Those conference sermon Diffner is responding to.

related.title: Todd Fisher Elected President of OK Baptists: A Step Toward or Away From Abolition?
related.reason: Tracks Blake Gideon's earlier role opposing abolitionism inside the BGCO, helpful background for his cited reversal.

related.title: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
related.reason: Long-form rebuttal to leading incrementalist arguments, expanding on the same Scripture-vs-pragmatism contrast Diffner draws.

related.title: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
related.reason: Situates Gideon's pulpit shift within the wider Baptist trajectory toward abolitionism that Diffner is celebrating.
